title: A Tutorial for Characterizing Transmon Qubits
authors: Alexandre M. Souza, Davi A. D. Chaves, Carmem M. Gilardoni, Roberto S. Sarthour, João P. Sinnecker, Ivan S. Oliveira
abstract: Superconducting transmon qubits are a leading technology for quantum information processing, yet their reliable operation rests on meticulous calibration and characterization routines. These processes have been fine-tuned and are relatively well understood by the quantum computing community. Nevertheless, it is often challenging for newcomers to compile all the available information into a practical experimental flow. In this tutorial, we present a comprehensive walkthrough for the characterization and optimization of tunable transmon qubits, demonstrated on a commercial five-qubit processor. Moving beyond theoretical description, we detail in a straightforward manner the complete workflow, from cryogenic setup and wiring to parametric amplifier optimum operation, flux sweet-spot identification, pulse calibration, and readout optimization. We also demonstrate the characterization of qubit-qubit coupling, covering all steps before multiqubit operations. This guide serves as a reference for experimentalists seeking to efficiently bring up transmon-based quantum devices.
summary: This tutorial serves as an essential experimental guide for characterizing superconducting transmon qubits. It walks the reader through the entire process, covering everything from initial setup and single-qubit spectroscopy to advanced measurements of anharmonicity, readout fidelity, and qubit-qubit coupling. The paper is crucial for experimentalists aiming to bring up and operate transmon-based quantum processors.
main_problem: The paper provides a comprehensive, practical, and step-by-step guide for experimentalists to characterize and optimize tunable transmon qubits.
main_result: It details the entire workflow, from cryogenic setup and flux sweet-spot identification to single-shot readout and qubit-qubit coupling characterization.
method: The methodology involves a combination of microwave spectroscopy (one-tone/two-tone), dispersive readout measurements, and advanced error characterization techniques like dynamical decoupling and composite pulses.
model_or_system: The physical system is superconducting transmon qubits, whose dynamics are modeled using Hamiltonians that approximate weakly anharmonic quantum oscillators. The characterization relies on mapping bosonic operators to Pauli operators in the two-level approximation.
key_observables: Qubit transition frequency ($\omega_q$), anharmonicity ($\alpha$), readout fidelity ($f$), effective coupling strength ($J$), and coherence times ($T_1, T_2$).
important_parameters: Operating regimes include the dispersive regime ($g \ll \Delta$), and key parameters involve flux bias ($\Phi$), detuning ($\Omega$), and pulse parameters (amplitude, duration).
main_assumptions: The procedures are highly dependent on the specific chip layout, fabrication details, and measurement hardware, and many advanced techniques rely on the qubit being well-described by the two-level approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the qubit energy level structure, Bloch sphere representations, resonator response vs. probe power (bare vs. dressed frequency), qubit spectroscopy vs. flux bias (showing the sweet spot), and fidelity decay curves comparing standard vs. composite pulses.
paper_structure: The tutorial progresses logically: first covering basic Hamiltonian models and setup; then detailing single-qubit control (Rabi oscillations, anharmonicity); followed by readout optimization (dispersive coupling, heterodyne detection); and finally addressing multi-qubit interactions and error mitigation (coupling strength $J$, composite pulses, DD).
why_interesting: For a theoretical physicist, this paper provides a rich experimental realization of open quantum systems dynamics, offering concrete measurements of decoherence rates ($T_1, T_2$) and the practical application of error mitigation protocols like dynamical decoupling and composite pulses.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Overview of transmon qubits. (a) Schematic view of the five qubits Soprano-D2 chip. (b) Schematics of a typical chip containing two coupled transmon qubits, labeled Q2 and Q4 in accordance with panel (a). Panel (b) highlights in different colors all essential components for qubit control and readout. Contact pads at one or both ends of different transmission lines identify the components which are electrically connected to electronics outside of the chip. (c) Circuit representation of a capacitively shunted Josephson junction. (d) Energy level structure comparing a harmonic oscillator (dotted line) and a transmon (red solid line).
FIG. 2. Bloch sphere representation of a qubit. Each point on the surface of the sphere represents a different quantum state.

FIG. 3. Representation of the experimental setup used in the exper- iments, highlighting the different temperature stages of the dilution refrigerator and all low-temperature electronic components. Tem- perature stages where attenuation is not required are connected us- ing 0 dB attenuators for thermal anchoring and impedance matching; these components are not explicitly shown in the figure.

FIG. 4. Calibration of traveling-wave parametric amplifier. (a) Signal amplification as a function of pump pulse amplitude and frequency. Optimal operation is achieved at the intersection of the highlighted black lines. (b) The average gain of the amplifier in the 7-8 GHz band as a function of the input signal amplitude. The gain was calcu- lated comparing the output signal with optimized amplification and without amplification.

FIG. 6. Readout probe frequency versus flux bias. The initial probe frequency and amplitude are chosen to enable dispersive resonator- qubit interaction with zero flux bias. The maximum readout fre- quency gives a first approximation for the qubit flux sweet spot.
